title: Conditional Sentences and the Imperfect Subjunctive
description: Master the three types of si-clauses (real, hypothetical, and unreal-past) and meet the imperfect subjunctive (hablara/hablase, tuviera, fuera) for the first time. Learn why English 'if I had' and 'if I were' become the imperfect subjunctive, why 'would' is the conditional, and the golden rule: never put the present subjunctive after si.

cards.front: hablaron → hablara; comieron → comiera; vivieron → viviera
cards.back: Build the imperfect subjunctive from the 3rd-plural preterite stem
cards.notes: Take the **ellos** preterite (*hablaron, comieron, vivieron*), drop **-ron**, and add the endings *-ra, -ras, -ra, -ramos, -rais, -ran*. This single rule captures every irregularity automatically.

cards.front: hablara = hablase; comiera = comiese; fuera = fuese
cards.back: Two interchangeable sets of endings: -ra and -se
cards.notes: The imperfect subjunctive has **two forms** that mean the same thing: *-ra* (*hablara, comiera, fuera*) and *-se* (*hablase, comiese, fuese*). The **-ra form is far more common**, especially in speech; *-se* is a touch more formal or literary.

cards.front: fueron → fuera; tuvieron → tuviera; pidieron → pidiera; pudieron → pudiera
cards.back: Irregular preterites carry their stem into the imperfect subjunctive
cards.notes: Because the rule starts from the *ellos* preterite, irregular stems come for free: *ser/ir → fueron → **fuera*** (both ser and ir share it), *tener → tuvieron → **tuviera***, *poder → pudieron → **pudiera***, *pedir → pidieron → **pidiera***, *hacer → hicieron → **hiciera***.

cards.back: The nosotros form takes a written accent
cards.notes: The **nosotros** form needs a written accent on the stem vowel: *habl**á**ramos, comi**é**ramos, fu**é**ramos, tuvi**é**ramos*. The *-se* versions accent the same way: *habl**á**semos*.

For **real or likely** conditions, the si-clause uses the **present indicative** and the result uses present, future, or an imperative: *Si tengo tiempo, te **ayudaré** / te **ayudo** / **ayúdame***. The si-clause stays in the indicative — **never** the subjunctive here.

cards.back: Type 2 (unreal present): Si + imperfect subjunctive, + conditional
cards.notes: For **hypothetical or unreal present** situations: *Si + imperfect subjunctive, + conditional*. *Si **tuviera** dinero, **viajaría** más* (If I **had** money, I **would** travel more). English 'if I had / if I were' = imperfect subjunctive; 'would' = conditional.

cards.back: Type 3 (unreal past): Si + pluperfect subjunctive, + conditional perfect
cards.notes: For an **unreal past** ('what would have happened'): *Si + **hubiera** + participle, + **habría** + participle*. *Si **hubiera estudiado**, **habría aprobado*** (If I had studied, I would have passed). The *-se* form *hubiese* also works.

cards.back: como si ('as if') always takes the imperfect subjunctive
cards.notes: **como si** ('as if') is always followed by the **imperfect subjunctive**, because it describes something contrary to fact: *Habla **como si fuera** experto* (He talks as if he were an expert). *Me trata como si **fuéramos** amigos*.

cards.back: Never use the present subjunctive directly after si
cards.notes: A classic error: **never** put the present subjunctive right after *si*. Say *Si **tengo*** (indicative, real) or *Si **tuviera*** (imperfect subjunctive, hypothetical) — but **never** *si tenga*. The present subjunctive simply does not follow *si* in conditional clauses.

cards.back: English 'would' in the result clause = Spanish conditional
cards.notes: The English **'would'** in the result of a hypothetical maps to the Spanish **conditional** (-ía endings): *viajar**ía**, ten**dría**, ser**ía***. Put the **imperfect subjunctive** in the si-clause and the **conditional** in the result: *Si **fuera** rico, **compraría** una casa*.

cards.front: If I were you → Si yo fuera tú / Yo en tu lugar
cards.back: English 'if I were' → imperfect subjunctive (fuera), never 'era'
cards.notes: English 'if I **were**' is the leftover English subjunctive; in Spanish it becomes the **imperfect subjunctive** *fuera*: *Si yo **fuera** tú, no lo haría* (If I were you, I wouldn't do it). Do **not** use the imperfect indicative *era* here.

cards.front: Viajaría más si tuviera dinero. = Si tuviera dinero, viajaría más.
cards.back: The two clauses can swap order; the comma drops when si comes second
cards.notes: You can lead with either clause. When the **si-clause comes first**, separate with a comma: *Si tuviera dinero, viajaría más*. When the **result comes first**, no comma: *Viajaría más si tuviera dinero*. The verb forms do not change.

steps.title: Meet the imperfect subjunctive
steps.body:
# Conditional sentences and the imperfect subjunctive

To talk about what *would* happen *if* something were true, Spanish needs a new tense: the **imperfect subjunctive**. The good news is the formation rule is simple and catches every irregularity.

## The rule: start from the 3rd-plural preterite

1. Take the **ellos** form of the preterite: *hablar → hablaron*, *comer → comieron*, *vivir → vivieron*.
2. Drop **-ron**.
3. Add the endings.

There are **two interchangeable sets** of endings — the **-ra** form (more common) and the **-se** form:

| | -ra form | -se form |
|---|---|---|
| yo | hablara | hablase |
| tú | hablaras | hablases |
| él/ella | hablara | hablase |
| nosotros | habláramos | hablásemos |
| vosotros | hablarais | hablaseis |
| ellos | hablaran | hablasen |

Note the **written accent** on *nosotros*: *habl**á**ramos / habl**á**semos*.

## Irregulars come for free

Because we start from the preterite, irregular stems carry over automatically:

- *ser / ir → fueron → **fuera / fuese*** (both verbs share it)
- *tener → tuvieron → **tuviera***
- *poder → pudieron → **pudiera***
- *pedir → pidieron → **pidiera***
- *hacer → hicieron → **hiciera***

> *comieron → comiera/comiese*; *fueron → fuera/fuese*; *tuvieron → tuviera*; *pidieron → pidiera*.

steps.title: The three types of si-clauses
steps.body:
## The three conditional types

Everything depends on **how real** the condition is:

| Type | Meaning | Si-clause | Result clause | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 — Real** | likely / open | si + **present indicative** | present / **future** / imperative | *Si tengo tiempo, te **ayudaré**.* |
| **2 — Hypothetical** | unreal present | si + **imperfect subjunctive** | **conditional** | *Si **tuviera** dinero, **viajaría** más.* |
| **3 — Unreal past** | impossible past | si + **pluperfect subjunctive** (hubiera + participle) | **conditional perfect** (habría + participle) | *Si **hubiera estudiado**, **habría aprobado**.* |

## Type 1 — real conditions

For something likely or open, keep the si-clause in the **present indicative**:

- *Si **tengo** tiempo, te **ayudaré**.* (future result)
- *Si **tengo** tiempo, te **ayudo**.* (present result)
- *Si **tienes** tiempo, **ayúdame**.* (imperative result)

## Type 2 — hypothetical / unreal present

For things that are **contrary to fact now** or unlikely: *si + imperfect subjunctive, + conditional*.

- *Si **tuviera** dinero, **viajaría** más.* (If I **had** money, I **would** travel more.)
- *Si yo **fuera** tú, no lo **haría**.* (If I **were** you, I **wouldn't** do it.)

English 'if I **had** / if I **were**' = imperfect subjunctive. English '**would**' = the Spanish **conditional**.

steps.title: Unreal past, como si, and the big pitfall
steps.body:
## Type 3 — the unreal past

To say what **would have happened** if the past had been different, stack two perfect forms:

- **Si + hubiera + participle, + habría + participle**
- *Si **hubiera estudiado**, **habría aprobado**.* (If I had studied, I would have passed.)
- *Si **hubieras llamado**, te **habría esperado**.*

The *-se* version *hubiese* is equally correct: *Si hubiese estudiado...*

## como si — 'as if'

**como si** is always followed by the **imperfect subjunctive**, because it always describes something untrue:

- *Habla **como si fuera** experto.* (He talks as if he were an expert.)
- *Me trata **como si fuéramos** amigos.*

## The golden rule: NEVER use the present subjunctive after si

This is the classic English-speaker error. After *si* in a condition, you use only:

- **present indicative** for a real condition → *Si **tengo***
- **imperfect subjunctive** for a hypothetical → *Si **tuviera***

You never write the **present subjunctive** directly after *si*:

- Right: *Si **tengo** tiempo...* / *Si **tuviera** tiempo...*
- Wrong: ~~*Si **tenga** tiempo...*~~

steps.title: Match the type to its structure

exercise.prompt: Match each conditional type with the verb structure it uses.
Match each infinitive with its yo imperfect-subjunctive (-ra) form.
Translate: *If I had money, I would travel more.* (Type 2: imperfect subjunctive + conditional)
Translate: *If I were you, I wouldn't do it.* (if I were → fuera, not era)
accept: Si yo fuera tú, no lo haría.
si yo fuera tu, no lo haria
Si yo fuera tú, no lo haria.
Si fuera tú, no lo haría.
Si yo fuese tú, no lo haría.
Si yo fuera tú, no lo haría
exercise.hint: 'if I were' = si yo fuera; 'wouldn't do' = no lo haría (conditional of hacer).
distractors: Si yo era tú, no lo haría.
Si yo sea tú, no lo haría.
Si yo fuera tú, no lo hacía.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the present indicative of *tener* (yo). Type 1 keeps the indicative after si.
exercise.sentence: Si ___ tiempo mañana, te ayudaré con la mudanza.
exercise.hint: Real condition → si + present indicative. NOT the subjunctive 'tenga'.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imperfect subjunctive of *tener* (yo) for this hypothetical.
exercise.sentence: Si ___ más tiempo libre, aprendería a tocar el piano.
exercise.hint: tuvieron → drop -ron → tuviera (or tuviese). The result 'aprendería' is conditional.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the imperfect subjunctive of *ser* after 'como si'.
exercise.sentence: Me habla como si ___ mi jefe, pero solo es un compañero.
exercise.hint: como si always takes the imperfect subjunctive; ser → fuera (or fuese).

steps.title: Build: If I had studied, I would have passed

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *If I had studied, I would have passed.* (Type 3, unreal past)

steps.title: Build: If I were rich, I would buy a house

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *If I were rich, I would buy a house.* (Type 2)
exercise.hint: 'if I were' = si fuera; 'would buy' = compraría (conditional).

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'If I have time, I'll call you'? (real condition)
exercise.hint: Real condition → si + present indicative (tengo); never 'si tenga'.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *He talks as if he were an expert.* (como si + imperfect subjunctive)
accept: Habla como si fuera un experto.
exercise.hint: como si + imperfect subjunctive; ser → fuera.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the auxiliary for the conditional-perfect result (habría/habrías... for tú).
exercise.sentence: Si hubieras llamado antes, te ___ esperado en la estación.
exercise.hint: Result of a Type 3 = conditional perfect: habría + participle (esperado).
